abstract: This article examines the operational reality of the Pattani Central Mosque to provide a counter-narrative to the prevailing discourse surrounding mosques in Southern Thailand. In recent years, these sites of worship have often been characterized as breeding grounds for Islamic radicalization and recruitment hubs for young militants opposing the Thai government. While the tragic 2004 Kru-ze Mosque incident highlighted a failure to maintain religious sites as "sanctuaries from violence," this study investigates the actual nature of contemporary Islamic teaching and mosque management. By analyzing the perspectives of key stakeholders—including Imams, preachers, and the congregation—the research explores local attitudes toward good governance, human rights, and the prevention of extremism. Ultimately, the findings suggest that the Pattani Central Mosque is defined by a balanced synthesis of moderate and conservative religious perspectives, rather than the radical or extremist tendencies frequently attributed to the region.
